exercises.title: Auth Code vs Client Credentials — pick the right flow
exercises.instructions:
Pick the correct OAuth2 flow:
A: Quicko tax app reading user's Zerodha trade history
B: Razorpay reconciliation service calling HDFC settlements API (no user)
C: User logging into Flipkart with Google account
D: ICICI Bank system calling Razorpay payment API every minute
exercises.expected_solution:
A: AUTHORIZATION CODE FLOW
   Quicko needs user's consent to read Zerodha data.
   User must log in and click 'Allow Quicko to read my trades'.
   Token represents the user's delegated permission.

B: CLIENT CREDENTIALS FLOW
   No user involved — automated service-to-service call.
   Razorpay reconciliation service authenticates as itself.
   Runs at 2am with no human present.

C: AUTHORIZATION CODE FLOW (with PKCE for browser)
   User is actively logging in — must be redirected to Google login.
   Google issues tokens after user authenticates.
   Flipkart receives an id_token with user email/profile.

D: CLIENT CREDENTIALS FLOW
   ICICI Bank's automated system — no user consent needed.
   ICICI authenticates as 'icici-bank-client' with client_id + secret.
   Gets a scoped token: payments:initiate only.

test_cases.expected:
A: AUTHORIZATION CODE FLOW
   Quicko needs user's consent to read Zerodha data.
   User must log in and click 'Allow Quicko to read my trades'.
   Token represents the user's delegated permission.

B: CLIENT CREDENTIALS FLOW
   No user involved — automated service-to-service call.
   Razorpay reconciliation service authenticates as itself.
   Runs at 2am with no human present.

C: AUTHORIZATION CODE FLOW (with PKCE for browser)
   User is actively logging in — must be redirected to Google login.
   Google issues tokens after user authenticates.
   Flipkart receives an id_token with user email/profile.

D: CLIENT CREDENTIALS FLOW
   ICICI Bank's automated system — no user consent needed.
   ICICI authenticates as 'icici-bank-client' with client_id + secret.
   Gets a scoped token: payments:initiate only.

hints: Auth Code: user must be present and consent
Client Credentials: service-to-service, no human involved

exercises.title: Complete the scope-based @PreAuthorize
exercises.instructions: Fill in to check OAuth2 scope 'payments:read'.

blanks.hint: Spring Security prefixes OAuth2 scopes with SCOPE_

hints: SCOPE_ prefix: Spring Security adds this automatically when mapping OAuth2 scopes
hasRole checks ROLE_ prefix; hasAuthority checks exact string

exercises.title: Configure OAuth2 Client Credentials in application.yml
exercises.instructions:
Write the application.yml OAuth2 client config for:
1. Client: 'hdfc-api' (client_credentials)
2. Scopes: payments:read, settlements:write
3. Token URI from HDFC Bank
4. Credentials from environment variables
exercises.expected_solution:
# application.yml:
spring:
  security:
    oauth2:
      client:
        registration:
          hdfc-api:
            client-id: ${HDFC_CLIENT_ID}           # from env var
            client-secret: ${HDFC_CLIENT_SECRET}   # from env var (never hardcode!)
            authorization-grant-type: client_credentials
            scope:
              - payments:read
              - settlements:write
        provider:
          hdfc-api:
            token-uri: https://api.hdfcbank.com/oauth2/token

# Spring Security:
# 1. On first WebClient call with 'hdfc-api' registration
# 2. POST https://api.hdfcbank.com/oauth2/token
#    grant_type=client_credentials
#    client_id=${HDFC_CLIENT_ID}
#    client_secret=${HDFC_CLIENT_SECRET}
#    scope=payments:read settlements:write
# 3. Response: { access_token, expires_in, token_type }
# 4. Token cached until near-expiry, then auto-refreshed
hints: client_credentials: the grant type for machine-to-machine auth
provider.token-uri: where to POST to get a token
Never hardcode client-secret — always use ${ENV_VAR}

exercises.title: WebClient with OAuth2 Client Credentials
exercises.instructions:
Write @Configuration that creates a WebClient for HDFC Bank API:
1. Uses OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager
2. Automatically injects Bearer token
3. Uses 'hdfc-api' client registration
4. Base URL from application property
hints: setDefaultClientRegistrationId: ties all requests from this WebClient to one OAuth2 registration
OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager: handles token acquisition, caching, refresh
filter(oauth2Filter): intercepts every request and adds the Bearer token

exercises.title: Resource server with scope-based security
exercises.instructions:
Write SecurityFilterChain for Razorpay partner API:
1. GET /api/payments → SCOPE_payments:read
2. POST /api/payments → SCOPE_payments:write
3. GET /api/settlements → SCOPE_settlements:read
4. /actuator/health → permitAll
5. @WebMvcTest (2 tests): correct scope → 200, wrong scope → 403
hints: hasAuthority('SCOPE_payments:read'): exact string match — SCOPE_ prefix required
Different scopes for GET vs POST: fine-grained API access control
.jwt(j->{}): minimal JWT resource server (uses application.yml for JWK URI)

exercises.title: Extract OAuth2 token metadata in a service
exercises.instructions:
Write TokenInspectorService:
1. Extract scopes from Jwt
2. Check if a specific scope is present
3. Get token expiry
4. Get client_id (sub claim for Client Credentials tokens)
5. @SpringBootTest: scope check works
hints: scope claim: space-separated string OR string list — handle both formats
azp (authorized party): identifies the client in Auth Code tokens
Jwt.withTokenValue(): test builder for Jwt objects without real token issuer

exercises.title: Service-to-service token propagation
exercises.instructions:
Razorpay payment service calls the fraud service, forwarding the user's token:
1. Extract JWT from incoming request's SecurityContext
2. Pass it as Bearer token to the downstream fraud service call
3. WebClient builder: forward the token
4. Explain: why token propagation (not Client Credentials) here
hints: JwtAuthenticationToken: Spring Security's Auth type for JWT-based auth
getToken().getTokenValue(): the raw JWT string for forwarding
Token propagation: preserves user identity downstream; Client Credentials: loses it

exercises.title: OAuth2 login with Google (social login)
exercises.instructions:
Configure Spring Boot for Google OAuth2 login:
1. application.yml: google client registration
2. SecurityFilterChain: oauth2Login() for web app
3. Controller: GET /profile using OidcUser
4. Explain: OIDC (OpenID Connect) vs plain OAuth2
5. @WebMvcTest: authenticated user gets profile
hints: oauth2Login(): Spring Security handles the full Auth Code + redirect flow
OidcUser: use for OIDC (scope=openid); OAuth2User for plain OAuth2
Google provider: built-in to Spring Security — just provide client-id and secret

exercises.title: Multi-tenant OAuth2: Zerodha + NSE + BSE APIs
exercises.instructions:
Write multi-client OAuth2 config:
1. Three clients: zerodha-auth, nse-api, bse-api (all client_credentials)
2. WebClient beans for each, named accordingly
3. OrderExecutionService: uses nse or bse client based on exchange
4. @SpringBootTest: service selects correct client
hints: @Qualifier: disambiguates multiple WebClient beans of same type
ServerOAuth2AuthorizedClientExchangeFilterFunction: reactive-compatible OAuth2 filter
Each WebClient uses a separate OAuth2 registration → separate token cache

exercises.title: What is wrong with this OAuth2 implementation?
exercises.expected_solution:
PROBLEMS:

1. Manual token fetching on EVERY request:
   Every call to check() makes a POST to /token first.
   This doubles latency (extra network call per request).
   Fix: Use OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager — it caches and auto-refreshes tokens.

2. client_secret in application.yml (even via @Value):
   application.yml is often committed to git.
   Fix: Use ${CLIENT_SECRET} env var, and store in Vault or Kubernetes Secret.
   Never have credentials in config files that touch version control.

3. Missing client_id in token request:
   POST /token without client_id → authentication server likely rejects.
   Fix: Include client_id AND client_secret (or use HTTP Basic auth header).

4. Wrong token forwarded to fraud service:
   The code gets a CLIENT CREDENTIALS token (service identity)
   then forwards it as the user's token.
   Fraud service receives a token for 'payment-service', not the merchant.
   Fix: Either use SecurityContext to get the merchant's token,
   or use Client Credentials intentionally and include merchant_id as a claim.

5. No error handling:
   If /token call fails: NullPointerException on .getAccessToken().
   If fraud service returns non-2xx: WebClient throws WebClientResponseException.
   Fix: Add fallback + circuit breaker (M19 patterns).

test_cases.expected:
PROBLEMS:

1. Manual token fetching on EVERY request:
   Every call to check() makes a POST to /token first.
   This doubles latency (extra network call per request).
   Fix: Use OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager — it caches and auto-refreshes tokens.

2. client_secret in application.yml (even via @Value):
   application.yml is often committed to git.
   Fix: Use ${CLIENT_SECRET} env var, and store in Vault or Kubernetes Secret.
   Never have credentials in config files that touch version control.

3. Missing client_id in token request:
   POST /token without client_id → authentication server likely rejects.
   Fix: Include client_id AND client_secret (or use HTTP Basic auth header).

4. Wrong token forwarded to fraud service:
   The code gets a CLIENT CREDENTIALS token (service identity)
   then forwards it as the user's token.
   Fraud service receives a token for 'payment-service', not the merchant.
   Fix: Either use SecurityContext to get the merchant's token,
   or use Client Credentials intentionally and include merchant_id as a claim.

5. No error handling:
   If /token call fails: NullPointerException on .getAccessToken().
   If fraud service returns non-2xx: WebClient throws WebClientResponseException.
   Fix: Add fallback + circuit breaker (M19 patterns).

hints: OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager: handles token caching — never fetch manually per request
client_secret in .yml: security risk if committed to git
Wrong token: service token ≠ user token

exercises.title: Complete OAuth2 resource server with scopes and roles
exercises.instructions:
Build Razorpay partner API resource server:
1. Payments endpoint: SCOPE_payments:read (scope check)
2. Admin endpoint: hasRole('ADMIN') (role check)
3. Custom JwtConverter: extract both roles AND scopes
4. @WebMvcTest (3 tests): scope OK, role OK, mixed missing → 403
5. All 3 pass
hints: Custom converter: add both ROLE_ and SCOPE_ authorities from different claims
SCOPE_payments:read and ROLE_ADMIN can coexist in the same JWT
jwt().authorities(): override all authorities for the test JWT

exercises.title: Token exchange: Zerodha service chain
exercises.instructions:
Zerodha's order-service calls risk-service, which calls market-data-service. Design token propagation through the chain:
1. order-service: receives user JWT, propagates to risk-service
2. risk-service: receives propagated JWT, calls market-data with its own Client Credentials token
3. Document: when to propagate vs when to issue new token
4. @SpringBootTest: token routing logic correct
hints: Propagate: when downstream needs user identity for authorization/audit
Client Credentials: when downstream provides generic service (no per-user data)
SecurityContextHolder: extract the current user's JWT for propagation

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay OAuth2 partner integration
exercises.instructions:
Build complete OAuth2 for Razorpay partner API:
1. SecurityFilterChain: scope-based access (payments:read, payments:write)
2. WebClient for outbound HDFC API calls (Client Credentials)
3. TokenInspectorService: extract scopes and expiry
4. @WebMvcTest (3 tests): scope checks work
5. All 3 pass
hints: GET scope ≠ POST scope: read vs write scopes separate
SCOPE_payments:read on POST → 403 (correct — need write scope)
@EnableMethodSecurity required for @PreAuthorize

exercises.title: OAuth2 token refresh handling
exercises.instructions:
Handle token expiry gracefully in a WebClient call:
1. Configure refresh_token grant in OAuth2 client
2. On 401 from upstream: auto-retry with refreshed token
3. If refresh fails: throw ServiceUnavailableException
4. Log: token refreshed at what time
5. @SpringBootTest: retry logic test
hints: Client Credentials: no refresh token — just request a new one (Spring does this auto)
Auth Code: refresh_token grant used to get new access_token without user re-login
OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager + refreshToken() provider: auto-refresh before expiry

exercises.title: Complete M23 T2 OAuth2 synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Zerodha OAuth2 stack synthesis:
1. SecurityFilterChain: scope-based (trades:read, trades:write)
2. TokenRoutingService: PROPAGATE_USER for risk, CLIENT_CREDENTIALS for market-data
3. TokenInspectorService: hasScope() method
4. @WebMvcTest + @SpringBootTest (3 tests): scopes, routing logic, inspector

All 3 tests pass.
hints: @WebMvcTest and @SpringBootTest in same file: both run independently
Jwt.withTokenValue builder: create test JWTs without real issuer
SCOPE_trades:read: must have SCOPE_ prefix for hasAuthority() check
